title: Design a System
description: Produce a pragmatic high-level design for a system or feature.
content:
Design a system for: {{requirements}}

Deliver:
1. The core components and how they interact (a simple diagram in text is fine).
2. The data model and the main data flows.
3. The key trade-offs you made and what you optimized for.
4. How it handles scale, failure, and the top 2 risks.

State your assumptions about scale, consistency, and budget. Prefer the simplest design that meets the requirements; note where you'd add complexity later if needed.

title: Evaluate Architecture Options
description: Compare candidate approaches against your real constraints.

title: Write an ADR
description: Capture an architecture decision in a clear, durable record.
content:
Write an Architecture Decision Record for this decision: {{decision}}

Use the standard structure:
- Title and status (proposed/accepted).
- Context: the forces and constraints that led here.
- Decision: what we're doing, stated plainly.
- Consequences: the good, the bad, and the newly-accepted risks.
- Alternatives considered and why they lost.

Keep it concise and honest — a future engineer should understand not just what we chose but why the obvious alternatives were rejected.

title: Design a Data Model
description: Model entities, relationships, and constraints for a domain.
content:
Design a data model for: {{domain}}

Provide: the entities and their fields with types, the relationships and cardinalities, and the key constraints/invariants. Call out where you'd index, where you'd denormalize and why, and how the model handles the trickiest query: {{key_query}}. Note any modeling decision that would be expensive to reverse later.

variables.name: key_query
variables.description: A demanding query it must serve

title: Plan an API Contract
description: Design a clean, evolvable API surface.
content:
Design the API for: {{service}}

Define the resources/operations, request and response shapes, error model, and pagination/filtering approach. Make it: consistent, hard to misuse, and evolvable without breaking clients. Specify versioning strategy and which fields are stable vs. experimental. Show the 2-3 most important calls with example payloads.

title: Identify Failure Modes
description: Stress-test a design by enumerating how it breaks.
content:
Here's my design/system: {{design}}

Do a failure-mode analysis. For each component and dependency, ask: what happens when it's slow, unavailable, returns garbage, or partially fails? Identify single points of failure, cascading-failure risks, and missing timeouts/retries/backpressure. Rank the failure modes by likelihood x impact and tell me the top three to address first.

title: Plan a Migration
description: Design a safe, incremental migration path.
content:
I need to migrate from {{current}} to {{target}}.

Design an incremental, reversible path: the sequence of steps, how to run old and new in parallel (and reconcile them), how to migrate data safely, and the rollback plan at each stage. Identify the riskiest step and how to de-risk it. Avoid any big-bang cutover unless you can justify it.

title: Decompose into Services or Modules
description: Find good boundaries for splitting a system.
content:
Help me decompose this system into modules/services: {{system}}

Find boundaries along the seams of change and ownership, not just nouns. For each proposed module: its single responsibility, what it owns, its interface, and its dependencies. Watch for chatty boundaries and shared databases. Tell me honestly whether this should stay a monolith with clean internal modules instead of being split.

title: Estimate Capacity and Cost
description: Sanity-check scale and cost before building.
content:
For this system: {{system}}

Do a back-of-the-envelope estimate. From the expected load ({{load}}), work out requests per second, storage growth, bandwidth, and the rough infrastructure cost. Identify which resource becomes the bottleneck first and at what scale. Flag any number that's off by an order of magnitude from what I assumed. Keep the math visible so I can challenge it.

title: Choose Sync vs Async
description: Decide whether work should be synchronous or queued.
content:
Should this be synchronous or asynchronous: {{operation}}

Decide based on: latency the caller can tolerate, whether the work can fail and be retried, ordering needs, and backpressure. If async, specify the queue, the retry and dead-letter policy, idempotency, and how the caller learns the result. If sync is fine, say so and avoid the complexity. Name the failure mode each choice introduces.

title: Design for Idempotency
description: Make an operation safe to retry.
content:
This operation may be retried or duplicated: {{operation}}

Design it to be idempotent so duplicates do no harm. Choose the idempotency key, where to store the dedup record, and how long to keep it. Handle the race where two identical requests arrive at once. Show what happens on retry-after-partial-failure. Note any step that's inherently non-idempotent and how to fence it.

title: Define Service Boundaries
description: Specify what a service owns and how others talk to it.
content:
For this service: {{service}}

Define its boundary precisely: the data it owns (and that no one else writes), the operations it exposes, and the events it emits. Specify the contract: request and response shapes, error semantics, and consistency guarantees. Identify what it must NOT know about its callers. Flag any shared database or leaky coupling that would undermine the boundary.
